sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Memory embeddings are crucial for memory-augmented systems, such as OpenClaw, but their evaluation is underexplored in current text embedding benchmarks, which narrowly focus on traditional passage retrieval and fail to assess models' ability to handle long-horizon memory retrieval tasks involving fragmented, context-dependent, and temporally distant information. To address this, we introduce the Long-horizon Memory Embedding Benchmark (LMEB), a comprehensive framework that evaluates embedding models' capabilities in handling complex, long-horizon memory retrieval tasks. LMEB spans 22 datasets and 193 zero-shot retrieval tasks across 4 memory types: episodic, dialogue, semantic, and procedural, with both AI-generated and human-annotated data. These memory types differ in terms of level of abstraction and temporal dependency, capturing distinct aspects of memory retrieval that reflect the diverse challenges of the real world. We evaluate 15 widely used embedding models, ranging from hundreds of millions to ten billion parameters. The results reveal that (1) LMEB provides a reasonable level of difficulty; (2) Larger models do not always perform better; (3) LMEB and MTEB exhibit orthogonality. This suggests that the field has yet to converge on a universal model capable of excelling across all memory retrieval tasks, and that performance in traditional passage retrieval may not generalize to long-horizon memory retrieval. In summary, by providing a standardized and reproducible evaluation framework, LMEB fills a crucial gap in memory embedding evaluation, driving further advancements in text embedding for handling long-term, context-dependent memory retrieval. LMEB is available at https://github.com/KaLM-Embedding/LMEB.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Memory embeddings are foundational to wide range of advanced applications, including agentic systems [OpenClaw Contributors, 2026, Zheng et al., 2025, Fang et al., 2025, Song et al., 2024] and evolving environments [Cao et al., 2025, Ouyang et al., 2025, Chen et al., 2025b]. These memoryaugmented systems [Du et al., 2025b] require sophisticated mechanisms to store, retrieve, update, and reason over vast amounts of memories, with retrieval being central to their effectiveness [Roediger III and Abel, 2022]. However, despite its importance, effective evaluation of memory embeddings remains underexplored in their capacity to handle long-horizon, context-rich memory retrieval tasks. Current evaluation benchmarks for text embeddings primarily focus on tasks that assess traditional passage retrieval [Thakur et al., 2021, Muennighoff et al., 2023, Xiao et al., 2024, Enevoldsen et al., 2025]. These benchmarks do not adequately evaluate embedding models capacity to handle long-horizon memory retrieval. Unlike passage retrieval that focuses on well-organized information, long-horizon memory retrieval tasks involve recalling fragmented, context-dependent information Corresponding Author over extended periods [Wu et al., 2025, Huet et al., 2025, Kohar and Krishnan, 2025], which current benchmarks fail to evaluate effectively. The lack of comprehensive evaluation protocol for assessing the capacity of embedding models to handle complex, long-term memory retrieval leaves significant gap in understanding how these models perform in practical, memory-intensive scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Long-horizon Memory Embedding Benchmark (LMEB) to provide unified, comprehensive evaluation framework aimed at advancing the development of embedding models capable of handling complex, long-horizon memory retrieval tasks. Building on the evaluation standards established for text embeddings, such as MTEB [Muennighoff et al., 2023], LMEB extends this evaluation protocol to memory retrieval tasks. LMEB spans diverse range of memory types, categorized into (i) Episodic, (ii) Dialogue,(iii) Semantic, and (iv) Procedural Memory [Du et al., 2025a]. Each of these memory types captures distinct aspects of memory retrieval, reflecting the varying needs of real-world scenarios. We categorize these memory types to ensure comprehensive evaluation of memory retrieval for embedding models: Episodic Memory involves the retrieval of past events linked to temporal cues, entities, contents, and spatial contexts [Fountas et al., 2024, Pink et al., 2025]. The ability to effectively retrieve and utilize episodic memories is critical for enhancing adaptability, decision-making, and temporal reasoning in complex, real-world tasks [Miao et al., 2024]. Dialogue Memory focuses on maintaining context across multi-turn interactions, enabling systems to recall previous dialogue turns and user preferences [Wu et al., 2025, Maharana et al., 2024]. This facilitates coherent conversations and improves the systems ability to adapt and provide personalized responses over time [Li et al., 2025, Du et al., 2024]. Semantic Memory involves recalling general knowledge and facts about the world, independent of time or specific context [Tulving et al., 1972]. Unlike episodic memory, semantic memory is stable, generalizable, and not tied to specific events. It forms the foundation for memory-augmented reasoning and adaptive knowledge utilization [Zhou et al., 2025]. Procedural Memory supports the retrieval of learned skills and action sequences, which are essential for tasks that require problem-solving and multi-step reasoning [Fang et al., 2025, Ouyang et al., 2025]. It is critical for automating and generalizing task-oriented experiences, especially in agentic systems and reinforcement learning systems [Lumer et al., 2025, Yu et al., 2025]. LMEB aims to provide clarity on how embedding models perform under broad spectrum of longhorizon memory retrieval demands and to serve as gateway to finding embedding models capable of handling long-horizon memory retrieval. To this end, LMEB consolidates diverse set of memory retrieval datasets and evaluation settings into single, standardized protocol, comprising 22 datasets across 4 memory types and 193 retrieval tasks. We release an open-source evaluation toolkit that enables the evaluation of new embedding models and the integration of new datasets with minimal effort, together with public leaderboard to facilitate reproducible comparisons and future progress2. We evaluate 15 widely used embedding models on LMEB, ranging from 10 billion parameters to several hundred million parameters. The results reveal several key findings: (1) LMEB provides reasonable level of difficulty. The top model achieved Mean (Dataset) score of 61.41 on N@10, indicating that LMEB offers meaningful challenge for evaluating memory retrieval. (2) Larger models do not always perform better. In some cases, larger models underperform smaller models, highlighting the significance of model architecture and task adaptability. (3) LMEB and MTEB are orthogonal. With Pearson and Spearman coefficients close to 0, LMEB focuses on long-horizon memory retrieval, while MTEB evaluates passage retrieval, demonstrating their complementary evaluation domains. Overall, LMEB provides both standardized yardstick for long-horizon memory retrieval and diagnostic tool for developing more reliable memory embedding models in real-world, memory-augmented systems.

sections.title: 2 The LMEB Benchmark
sections.content: In this section, we first provide an overview and taxonomy of LMEB (2.1), followed by detailed dataset and diversity analyses (2.2), an evaluation protocol and extensibility discussion (2.3), as well as construction details (2.4). 2LMEB Leaderboard: https://github.com/KaLM-Embedding/LMEB.github.io 2 Figure 1: Overview of LMEB memory categories and specificities. Table 1 presents detailed dataset statistics. Tables 5, 6, 7, and 8 provide examples of query-relevant document pairs for each dataset. Tables 10, 11, 12, and 13 provide detailed task types and example abilities assessed.

sections.title: 2.1 LMEB Overview and Taxonomy
sections.content: The Long-horizon Memory Embedding Benchmark (LMEB) is designed to provide comprehensive evaluation framework for embedding models, specifically targeting long-term memory retrieval tasks. Figure 1 and Table 1 summarize memory categories, memory specificities, and statistics of the datasets in LMEB. Unlike existing text embedding benchmarks [Thakur et al., 2021, Muennighoff et al., 2023, Xiao et al., 2024, Enevoldsen et al., 2025] mainly focusing on passage retrieval, LMEB is tailored to assess the scenarios involving recalling fragmented, context-dependent, and temporally distant memory information, thereby bridging significant flaw in current embedding benchmarks. In line with MTEB Muennighoff et al. [2023], the design of LMEB is driven by four key principles to ensure its effectiveness in evaluating embedding models for long-term memory retrieval tasks: (1) Generalization: LMEB emphasizes zero-shot evaluation, where models are assessed based on their previously learned embedding capabilities without task-specific fine-tuning. (2) Usability: To enhance accessibility, LMEB facilitates the seamless integration of new models and datasets. Model integration requires minimal code adjustments, while new datasets can be added via simple configuration files. (3) Diversity: LMEB encompasses wide range of memory types and tasks, including episodic, dialogue, semantic, and procedural memory, utilizing both AI-generated and human-annotated datasets. (4) Difficulty: LMEB incorporates tasks with varying levels of difficulty, considering factors such as granularity, corpus size, relevant documents per query, and query/document lengths. In total, LMEB includes 22 English zero-shot evaluation datasets, spanning 4 memory types and 193 retrieval tasks. Table 1 summarizes the detailed statistics of these datasets. LMEB covers four memory types: (i) Episodic, (ii) Dialogue,(iii) Semantic, and (iv) Procedural Memory [Du et al., 2025a]. Task diversity and difficulty vary across the datasets, with differences in Granularity, Querysrc, Corpussrc, #Corpus, Avg. / Q, and Avg. Word Lengths. For example, granularity differs across tasks, from event-level in episodic memory to turn, round, or session-level in dialogue one. Semantic memory usually deals with sentence or paragraph-level granularity, whereas procedural memory spans from item-level to trajectory-level. This diversity ensures that LMEB evaluates models across various aspects of memory retrieval. The data sources for queries and corpora vary significantly across tasks. Many episodic, dialogue, and procedural memory retrieval tasks incorporate mix of AI-generated and human-annotated data, ensuring that models are tested on both synthetic and real-world data. In contrast, semantic tasks predominantly rely on human-annotated content, ensuring genuine datasets for knowledge retrieval. This mix provides balanced evaluation of models on diverse range of data. In LMEB, we categorize memory into four types, characterized along two key dimensions, as shown in Figure 2: (1) Level of Abstraction, which differentiates between concrete, eventspecific memories (e.g., episodic memory) and more abstract memory representations (e.g., dialogue and procedural memory, where the former involves fragmented conversational turns and the latter pertains to generalized skills or procedures); and (2) Temporal Dependency, which refers to the extent to which memory retrieval relies on temporal context. Episodic and dialogue memories exhibit high temporal dependency due to their reliance on event sequences and interactions over time. Specifically, the characteristics of each memory type can be summarized as follows: (i) Episodic Memory has low abstraction and high temporal dependency, focusing on specific events and their order; (ii) Dialogue Memory shows high temporal dependency but is more abstract than episodic memory, involving the recall of fragmented conversational turns; (iii) Semantic Memory is low in both abstraction and temporal dependency, dealing with stable, general knowledge; and (iv) Procedural Memory combines high abstraction with low temporal dependency, focusing on generalized skills, action sequences, experiences, etc. Figure 2: Memory taxonomy of LMEB.

sections.title: 2.2 Dataset and Diversity Analysis
sections.content: Table 1 summarizes the LMEB datasets, which span diverse memory types and retrieval granularities, including event-, turn-, round-, session-, sentence-, paragraph-, tool-, and experience-level retrieval settings. Following BEIR [Thakur et al., 2021], we quantify inter-dataset diversity by computing pairwise weighted Jaccard Similarity (JS) [Ioffe, 2010] over unigram word distributions in the corpus of each dataset. We report the resulting similarities across all dataset pairs. The theoretical formulation of the weighted JS metric is provided in Appendix E. Additionally, we present 2D visualization of 4 (a) Heatmap of weighted JS scores. (b) 2D force-directed visualization. Figure 3: Inter-dataset diversity in LMEB. The left side illustrates pairwise weighted Jaccard Similarity (JS) scores between unigram word distributions of each dataset corpus, while the right side shows dataset relationships with force-directed 2D layout. dataset relationships using force-directed layout implemented in NetworkX [Hagberg et al., 2007], where nodes represent datasets and edge weights are proportional to their JS scores. Note that the 2D visualization only includes episodic, dialogue, and semantic datasets. Procedural datasets are omitted, as their corpora consistently yield low JS scores compared to the rest, causing them to appear as weakly connected outliers. From Figure 3, we have several insights into the inter-dataset diversity within LMEB: (1) As shown in Figure 3(a), dialogue datasets exhibit relatively high similarity due to shared conversational topics, while procedural datasets show low similarity, as they focus on domain-specific tasks such as code, planning, and tools. (2) TMD and LoCoMo are similar because TMD incorporates LoCoMos corpus. Additionally, PeerQA and QASPER demonstrate high similarity due to their shared use of academic natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML) papers. (3) Figure 3(b) visualizes these relationships with 2D force-directed layout, where datasets within the same memory type tend to cluster together. Overall, the analysis highlights the diversity of LMEB across memory types and retrieval datasets, providing comprehensive benchmark for long-horizon memory retrieval tasks.

sections.title: 2.3 Evaluation Protocol and Extensibility
sections.content: The evaluation protocol of LMEB is built on top of the MTEB v2 framework3. LMEB provides unified evaluation pipeline and extensive model wrappers, facilitating reproducible benchincluding Transformers [Wolf et al., 2020], marking across widely used model scaffolds, Sentence-Transformers [Reimers and Gurevych, 2019], and vLLM [Kwon et al., 2023]. To support scalable evaluation across heterogeneous memory sources, LMEB adopts standardized IR-style data format with four components: queries, corpus, qrels, and an optional candidates file. We convert all collected datasets into this unified format, ensuring consistent preprocessing, indexing, and evaluation across growing number of tasks. The optional candidates file restricts the retrieval scope when query defines bounded memory pool. For example, in dialogue settings, query is evaluated only against its corresponding conversation history, rather than the full corpus, ensuring evaluation under realistic access constraints. LMEB reports standard IR metrics, including Precision, Recall, MAP (Mean Average Precision), MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rate), and NDCG (Normalized Cumulative Discount Gain) for arbitrary topk. Following MTEB [Muennighoff et al., 2023] and BEIR [Thakur et al., 2021], we use NDCG@k as the main metric and report NDCG@10 by default, as it captures both ranking quality and graded relevance when multiple memories may be relevant. 3https://github.com/embeddings-benchmark/mteb 5 LMEB is designed for easy extensibility along two axes. First, new embedding models can be integrated with minimal code changes via pre-built model wrappers, , supporting both local inference and server-based deployment. Second, new datasets can be added by providing data files in the standardized corpus/queries/qrels format (and candidates if necessary). This design makes LMEB suitable for both academic research and industrial applications, where memory sources and evaluation needs are constantly evolving.

sections.title: 2.4 Construction Details
sections.content: LMEB converts all collected resources into unified IR-style schema consisting of queries, corpus, qrels, and an optional candidates file. The resulting files are as follows: queries.jsonl: list of queries. Each is associated with unique identifier and query text. Specifically, each entry contains unique id and text field. For queries containing relative time expressions, we append an explicit query-time anchor to the query text (e.g., What did we discuss 2 days ago? [Current time: 11:17 AM on Sunday 22 October, 2023]), to disambiguate temporal references. corpus.jsonl: The memory corpus to be retrieved from. Each memory item is represented by id, text, and title. For episodic and dialogue memories, timestamps are preserved in title and/or text to support time-sensitive queries (e.g., What type of group did Caroline join in July 2023?). For dialogue memories with session-, round-, or turn-level hierarchy, we encode the corresponding metadata in title, so queries targeting specific span (e.g., What did we discuss over sessions 1 through 3?) are evaluated on the intended memory scope. qrels.tsv: Relevance annotations between queries and memory items. This file records relevant <query, memory> pairs, each assigned relevance label of 1. candidates.jsonl (optional): bounded candidate pool for each query or scenario. For tasks with restricted memory scope, we provide candidates.jsonl, where each scene_id (either query id or shared scenario id) is mapped to list of admissible candidate_doc_ids. This constrains retrieval to the permitted memory space, such as conversation history rather than the full corpus. # queries.jsonl {"id": "query_id", "text": "query text"} ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # corpus.jsonl {"id": "corpus_id", "text": "corpus text", "title": "corpus title"} ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # qrels.tsv query_id ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # candidates.jsonl (optional) {"scene_id": "scene_id", "candidate_doc_ids": ["corpus_id", ......]} corpus_id

sections.title: Implementation Details
sections.content: Following [Thakur et al., 2021, Muennighoff et al., 2023], we evaluate retrieval performance using two widely adopted IR metrics: (i) NDCG@k (N@k) (main metric), which measures ranking quality with logarithmically discounting over the top-k results; and (ii) Recall@k (R@k), which measures the proportion of gold memories successfully retrieved within the top-K results. Note that the Recall@k metric refers to Capped Recall@k. The standard Recall@k can sometimes yield unintuitive results, especially when the number of relevant documents for query exceeds k. To address this issue, we cut off the recall score at k, indicating that if the number of relevant documents for query exceeds k, we use the minimum of and the total number of relevant documents to compute the recall score. We report results under two query settings: (i) w/o inst., where the model encodes the query alone; and (ii) w/ inst., where the model encodes the concatenation of the instruction and the query, allowing us to quantify the impact of instructions on retrieval performance. We use maximum input length of 1024 tokens for both queries and corpus passages. However, multilingual-e5-large-instruct and bge-large-en-v1.5 use maximum length of 512 tokens, as they only support this maximum length. 7 Figure 4: Performance comparison between w/o inst. and w/ inst.. The x-axis represents the two conditions (w/o inst. and w/ inst.), and the y-axis indicates the N@10 performance.

sections.title: 4 Main Results
sections.content: Table 2 and Table 3 present the performance of embedding models of various scales on LMEB. From the experimental results, we mainly have the following observations: LMEB Benchmark Offers Reasonable Level of Difficulty. The experimental results indicate that the LMEB benchmark presents reasonable level of difficulty for embedding models. The best-performing model (i.e., bge-multilingual-gemma2) achieves Mean (Dataset) score of 61.41 on N@10 under the w/ inst. setting, suggesting that the tasks are sufficiently challenging without being excessively difficult. The performance of the top models demonstrates that the LMEB strikes well-balanced level of task complexity, providing meaningful evaluation of embedding models capabilities to handle various types of episodic, dialogue, semantic, and procedural memories. Larger Embedding Models Do Not Always Perform Better. The experimental results show that larger embedding models, such as KaLM-Embedding-Gemma3 and bge-multilingual-gemma2, do not always outperform smaller models. For example, in the w/o inst. setting, models like KaLMEmbedding-Gemma3 and bge-multilingual-gemma2 perform worse than the smaller model, e.g., EmbeddingGemma-300M and bge-m3 (Dense). This indicates that larger models do not consistently provide better performance, and smaller models, such as EmbeddingGemma-300M, can sometimes achieve comparable or even superior results. This suggests that factors other than model size, such as model architecture, training data, and task adaptability, play key role in determining performance. The Impact of Task Instructions on Model Performance Varies. As shown in Figure 4, the effect of task instructions on model performance varies across different models. For some models, task instructions result in positive impact, while for others, there is little to no change, and in some cases, negative effect is observed. This indicates that different models exhibit distinct preferences for task instructions, with some models showing sensitivity to them, while others remain insensitive or even prefer to operate without them. The models that tend to prefer task instructions include KaLM-Embedding-Gemma3, bge-multilingual-gemma2, Qwen3-Embedding-8B, e5-mistral7b-instruct, Qwen3-Embedding-4B, Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B, multilingual-e5-large-instruct, KaLMEmbedding-V2.5, KaLM-Embedding-V1, and jina-v5-text-nano. On the other hand, NV-Embed-v2 and jina-v5-text-small show insensitivity to task instructions. Some models, such as bge-m3 (Dense), bge-large-en-v1.5, and EmbeddingGemma-300M, perform better without task instructions, indicating preference for operating without task instructions. This difference in task instruction preferences is mainly influenced by the training data and the training methods used for each embedding model. 8 Figure 5: Correlation between the evaluation scores on LMEB and MTEB (eng, v2) (retrieval subset) Enevoldsen et al. [2025]. The evaluation score for LMEB is based on the N@10 metric, aligned with MTEB (eng, v2) (retrieval subset). Mean (Dataset) scores under w/ inst. are used for LMEB. Note that bge-m3 (Dense), bge-large-en-v1.5, and EmbeddingGemma-300M perform better without task instructions, so their results are based on the w/o inst. setting. The size of the points in the plot is proportional to the size of the models.

sections.title: 5 Correlation Analysis
sections.content: Figure 5, Figure 6, Figure 7, Figure 8, and Figure 9 present the correlation between LMEB, LMEBEpisodic, LMEB-Dialogue, LMEB-Semantic, and LMEB-Procedural with the traditional passage retrieval benchmark, MTEB (eng, v2) (retrieval subset) Enevoldsen et al. [2025]. We computed Pearson and Spearman rank correlation coefficients to assess their relationships. From the results, we mainly have the following observations: LMEB and MTEB Exhibit Orthogonality in Evaluation Domains. From Figure 5, the correlation analysis between LMEB and MTEB (eng, v2) (retrieval subset) shows low Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients of -0.115 and -0.130, respectively, demonstrating that the two benchmarks are orthogonal in the domains they evaluate. While MTEB mainly focuses on traditional passage retrieval, LMEB is tailored to evaluate long-horizon memory retrieval, which requires handling fragmented, context-dependent, and temporally distant information. This orthogonality emphasizes the unique value of LMEB in assessing long-term memory retrieval capabilities, making it crucial benchmark for evaluating embedding models in complex, real-world, memory-intensive scenarios. MTEB Performance Does Not Generalize Well to LMEB-Episodic/Dialogue. Models that perform well on MTEB struggle to transfer or generalize to LMEB-Episodic/Dialogue, especially LMEBDialogue. For example, as shown in Figure 7, the Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients between LMEB-Dialogue and MTEB (eng, v2) (retrieval subset) are -0.496 and -0.364, respectively. This indicates that strong performance in traditional passage retrieval does not easily transfer to domains involving fragmented, redundant, and contextually coherent dialogue data. This further emphasizes the unique value of LMEB in evaluating embedding models for real-world scenarios, where effectively handling complex episodic and dialogue memory retrieval is crucial. MTEB Shows Partial Generalization to LMEB-Semantic/Procedural. MTEB shows some, but not substantial, generalization to LMEB-Semantic and LMEB-Procedural, as shown in Figures 8 and 9. The Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients between LMEB-Semantic and MTEB 9 are 0.103 and 0.061, respectively, while for LMEB-Procedural, they are 0.291 and 0.429. Although both LMEB-Semantic and MTEB evaluate semantic passage retrieval, their correlations are relatively weak. This is mainly because LMEB-Semantic focuses on retrieval within contextually coherent and bounded scenarios, whereas MTEB evaluates open-domain retrieval tasks. On the other hand, LMEBProcedural shows relatively stronger correlation with MTEB, likely because some embedding models are also trained on tool or code retrieval tasks, which overlap with some procedural memory retrieval tasks. In conclusion, while MTEB performance shows some level of transfer to LMEB, the distinct differences in domain focus and data characteristics result in different emphases in evaluating the capabilities of embedding models.

sections.title: 6.1 Embedding Benchmarks
sections.content: A long line of benchmarks has proposed evaluating text embeddings across downstream tasks, including retrieval, semantic textual similarity (STS), clustering, and classification. Early evaluations typically centered on small collection of sentence-level similarity tasks, e.g., SentEval [Conneau and Kiela, 2018] and SemEval [Agirre et al., 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, Afzal et al., 2016]. To broaden coverage, retrieval-focused benchmarks such as BEIR [Thakur et al., 2021] consolidate heterogeneous IR datasets to evaluate cross-domain generalization, while multilingual benchmarks such as MIRACL [Zhang et al., 2023] and AIR-Bench [Chen et al., 2025a] target multilingual retrieval. More recently, MTEB/C-MTEB/MMTEB [Muennighoff et al., 2023, Xiao et al., 2024, Enevoldsen et al., 2025] provide unified evaluation protocol and leaderboard for text embeddings across diverse tasks (e.g., retrieval, classification, clustering, reranking, and STS), substantially lowering the barrier to standardized evaluation and comparison. Meanwhile, multimodal embedding benchmarks evaluate image-text embeddings for retrieval and alignment. For example, MIEB [Xiao et al., 2025] and VLM2Vec-V1 [Jiang et al., 2025] provide unified evaluation protocol for image-text embeddings across diverse task categories. Overall, existing benchmarks have significantly advanced standardized evaluation for embedding quality. However, they rarely test long-horizon memory retrieval with fragmented, context-dependent, and temporally distant evidence, making it unclear how well embedding models support such memory-centric retrieval in practice.

sections.title: 6.2 Embedding Models
sections.content: Text embedding models map text into continuous vectors that enable efficient similarity search and support downstream tasks [Zhao et al., 2025b, Zhang et al., 2025a, Zhao et al., 2024]. Early approaches learned static word embeddings, such as GloVe [Pennington et al., 2014], and composed sentence representations via pooling, but lacked contextual awareness. Transformer architecture [Devlin et al., 2019] adds contextualization via self-attention and has become the dominant backbone for embeddings. High-quality sentence embeddings typically require task-specific fine-tuning, as Sentence-BERT [Reimers and Gurevych, 2019] showed that fine-tuning on sentence-pair objectives yields embeddings directly comparable with cosine similarity. Recently, adapting large language models (LLMs) into embedding models has become mainstream, such as GTE [Li et al., 2023, Zhang et al., 2024a], Qwen3-Embedding [Zhang et al., 2025b], BGE [Chen et al., 2024], Jina [Günther et al., 2025, Akram et al., 2026], NV-Embed [Lee et al., 2025a], and KaLM [Hu et al., 2025, Zhao et al., 2025a]. Common approaches include multi-task contrastive training [Mohr et al., 2024] to unify heterogeneous supervision and instruction tuning [Asai et al., 2023, Su et al., 2023] to condition embeddings on natural-language task descriptions. In parallel, multimodal embedding models [Zhang et al., 2024b, Jiang et al., 2025, Meng et al., 2025] adopt similar contrastive pretraining recipes to learn shared image-text representations for multimodal applications. While LLM-based embeddings often generalize well, they introduce practical trade-offs in efficiency, latency, and deployment cost, and improvements on standard semantic benchmarks may not transfer to long-horizon, context-dependent memory retrieval.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we presented the Long-horizon Memory Embedding Benchmark (LMEB) to evaluate embedding models on long-horizon, context-rich memory retrieval tasks. LMEB consists of 22 10 datasets covering 4 memory types and 193 retrieval tasks, filling critical gap in current benchmarks like MTEB, neglecting long-term, fragmented, context-dependent, and temporally distant memory information retrieval. We evaluate state-of-the-art embedding models on LMEB and uncover several key findings: (1) LMEB offers reasonable level of difficulty; (2) Even models that perform well on traditional passage retrieval benchmarks struggle with memory retrieval tasks; (2) LMEB and MTEB exhibit orthogonality in the capabilities they assess. By open-sourcing LMEB with standardized data format compatible with MTEB and easy-to-adapt code, we offer the research community unified, reproducible framework for evaluating embedding models in real-world memory retrieval tasks. LMEB not only advances current evaluation practices but also provides critical foundation for developing embedding models that can handle complex, real-world memory retrieval challenges. Through LMEB, we aim to drive further innovation in text embedding, offering insights to shape the next generation of memory-augmented systems.

sections.title: A Datasets
sections.content: Below, we list the 22 evaluation datasets in LMEB, spanning four memory types. Each dataset includes corpus and set of test queries. We provide dataset website links in Table 4. We describe each dataset below: A.1 Episodic Memory Retrieval Episodic memory retrieval aims to recall past events grounded in temporal cues, entities, contents, and spatial contexts [Fountas et al., 2024, Pink et al., 2025]. In LMEB, we treat an episodic query as input and retrieve the corresponding event memories as output. Examples are presented in Table 5. EPBench [Huet et al., 2025] is synthetic episodic memory benchmark for evaluating event recall and episodic reasoning in LLMs. It represents episodic events with structured fields, including temporal and spatial context, involved entities, and detailed descriptions. We use its event set as the corpus and the provided task queries as queries Q. KnowMeBench [Wu et al., 2026] is benchmark for long-horizon person understanding built from long-form autobiographical narratives. It converts each narrative into time-anchored, flashbackaware memory stream and evaluates evidence-linked queries spanning factual recall, subjective state attribution, and principle-level reasoning. We use the reconstructed narrative stream as the corpus and the benchmark questions as queries Q. A.2 Dialogue Memory Retrieval Dialogue memory retrieval aims to maintain context across multi-turn interactions by recalling relevant dialogue history and user preferences [Wu et al., 2025, Maharana et al., 2024]. Such recall supports coherent conversations and enables personalization over time [Du et al., 2024, Li et al., 2025]. In LMEB, we treat dialogue query as input and retrieve the corresponding dialogues as output. Examples are presented in Table 6. 17 Dataset EPBench Query Relevant-Document Think about Aurora Chavezs experiences. Describe all the key events theyve been involved in, focusing on what happened rather than when or where it occurred. . . . . . . Aurora implemented blockchain solutions with determination that bordered on obsession. . . . . . . It wasnt until Samara Bayes tapped her on the shoulder that Aurora Chavez realized how much time had passed. She blinked, momentarily disoriented as she emerged from her coding trance. Samara Bayes gestured towards the refreshment table, knowing smile on their face. . . . . . . KnowMeBench On August 15, 1969, which shipping companys bus did the narrators family take? { } "id": 1, "timestamp": "1969-08-15 14:00:30", "location": "Rocky path", "action": "I watched the vehicle cross bridge, driving down the narrow fjord with its right turn signal blinking before finally stopping.", (cid:44) "dialogue": null, "environment": "The bus belonged to Aucity (cid:44) (cid:44) (cid:44) Shipping Company, painted in two shades of brown.", (cid:44) "background": null, "inner_thought": null Granularity Event Event Table 5: Examples of query-relevant document pairs for episodic memory retrieval datasets. LoCoMo [Maharana et al., 2024] is dataset for very long-term, persona-grounded dialogues with It contains conversations of up to 35 sessions (300 turns; 9k explicit temporal event structure. tokens on average) and evaluates long-range dialogue memory via tasks such as question answering. We use the dialogue history as the corpus and the benchmark questions as queries Q. LongMemEval [Wu et al., 2025] is used for evaluating long-term memory in chat assistants under sustained, multi-session interactions. It probes five core abilities: information extraction, multisession reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, and abstention. We use the chat history as the corpus and the curated questions as queries Q. REALTALK [Lee et al., 2025b] consists of 21-day corpus of authentic dialogues, capturing long-term, open-domain interactions with richer emotional expressions and more varied persona consistency than synthetic data. It includes tasks like persona simulation and memory probing, where models must answer questions based on long-term dialogue. LMEB focuses on the memory-probing task, using the dialogue history as the corpus and the memory-probing questions as queries Q. TMD [Alonso et al., 2024] introduces dataset designed to evaluate retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) models in the context of long-term conversational memory. It focuses on two key challenges: time/event-based queries, which require retrieving information from previous conversations based on temporal cues or event order, and ambiguous queries, which necessitate surrounding conversational context to understand. In LMEB, we use this datasets conversation history as the corpus and the timeor context-dependent queries as queries Q. MemBench [Tan et al., 2025] introduces comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the memory capabilities of LLM-based agents. It incorporates multiple memory levels, such as factual and reflective memory, and evaluates agents in variety of interactive scenarios, including participation and observation. We use the dialogues from this dataset as the corpus and the associated queries as queries Q. ConvoMem [Pakhomov et al., 2025] introduces benchmark for conversational memory evaluation, containing 75,336 question-answer pairs across categories such as user facts, assistant recall, preferences, and temporal changes. LMEB samples 5,867 queries that focus on dialogue-based retrieval, highlighting the evolving nature of conversational memory, where memory systems grow progressively with each conversation. We use the dialogue history as the corpus and the sampled queries as queries Q. A.3 Semantic Memory Retrieval Semantic memory retrieval focuses on recalling general knowledge and concepts that are largely independent of time or specific context [Tulving et al., 1972]. Semantic memory is more stable and generalizable, and supports knowledge-augmented reasoning and use [Zhou et al., 2025]. In LMEB, we treat the question as input and retrieve the passage containing the answer as output. Examples are 18 presented in Table 7. For long-document datasets (i.e., LooGLE and NovelQA), we segment texts using semchunk5 with chunk size of 256. QASPER [Dasigi et al., 2021] is question-answering (QA) dataset grounded in full research papers, designed to reflect information-seeking queries that require reasoning across multiple document sections. It contains 5,049 questions over 1,585 NLP papers, where questions are written from the title and abstract and answered using evidence from the full text. In LMEB, we process the dataset and retain 1,335 valid queries. We use the paper content (segmented into passages) as the corpus and the QASPER questions as queries Q. NovelQA [Wang et al., 2025] evaluates long-context comprehension over complex narratives drawn from English novels. It features manually annotated questions targeting nuanced understanding (e.g., detail-oriented and multi-hop queries) under very long inputs, with average context lengths exceeding 200K tokens. We use the novel text (segmented into passages) as the corpus and the benchmark questions as queries Q. PeerQA [Baumgärtner et al., 2025] is document-level scientific QA dataset built from questions raised in peer reviews and answers provided by the original authors. It contains 579 QA pairs over 208 academic papers, with majority from ML and NLP, and supports evidence retrieval. In LMEB, we filter out questions without the corresponding paper text and retain 136 questions. We use the paper text (segmented into sentences) as the corpus and the PeerQA questions as queries Q. Covid-QA [Möller et al., 2020] is biomedical QA dataset annotated from COVID-19 scientific articles in CORD-19. It contains expert-written question-answer pairs, where annotators mark text as answer and formulate corresponding questions. In LMEB, we use the processed version released by [Conti et al., 2025]. We use the selected CORD-19 articles (segmented into passages) as the corpus and the annotated questions as queries Q. ESG-Reports [Macé et al., 2025] consists of lengthy ESG reports from the fast-food industry. It is derived from ViDoRe Benchmark v2 [Macé et al., 2025], which originally annotates query-page relevance for visual document retrieval. In LMEB, we use the processed version released by Conti et al. [2025], which converts documents to text, chunks them into passages (i.e., the corpus C), and manually re-annotates query (i.e., queries Q)-passage pairs after filtering queries that rely primarily on visual evidence (e.g., tables or graphs). MLDR [Chen et al., 2024] is multilingual long-document retrieval dataset constructed from Wikipedia, Wudao, and mC4 across 13 languages, where questions are generated from sampled paragraphs and paired with their source documents. In LMEB, we use only the English subset. We adopt the processed version Conti et al. [2025], where documents are chunked into passages (i.e., the corpus C), and GPT-4o is used to annotate the gold chunk within the corresponding document for each query (i.e., queries Q). LooGLE [Li et al., 2024] is long-context understanding benchmark built from post-2022 documents, with long inputs (often >24K tokens) and newly generated questions spanning diverse domains. It includes curated high-quality QA pairs that target long-dependency and short-dependenct reasoning. We use the chunked documents as the corpus and the generated questions as queries Q. SciFact [Wadden et al., 2020] is scientific claim verification dataset that requires retrieving evidence abstracts from the research literature to support or refute given claim. It contains 1.4K expertwritten claims paired with annotated evidence abstracts (with labels and rationales). In LMEB, we use the dev split (300 claims) since the test split is unlabeled, and after filtering claims with empty evidence, we retain 188 valid queries. We use the abstract collection as the corpus and the retained claims as queries Q. A.4 Procedural Memory Retrieval Procedural memory retrieval focuses on recalling learned skills, action patterns, and structured procedures that guide task execution and multi-step reasoning [Fang et al., 2025, Ouyang et al., 2025]. Such retrieval is essential for automating complex behaviors, supporting tool use, and enabling adaptive decision-making in reinforcement learning and tool-augmented systems [Lumer et al., 2025, Yu et al., 2025]. In LMEB, we treat task instruction or problem description as input and retrieve the 5https://github.com/isaacus-dev/semchunk 19 corresponding procedure, action sequence, or solution trajectory as output. Examples are presented in Table 8. Gorilla [Patil et al., 2024] introduces APIBench, benchmark covering APIs from HuggingFace, TorchHub, and TensorHub, designed to evaluate tool-use through API call generation. key component of Gorilla is the integration of document retriever, which enables models to fetch up-to-date API documentation and adapt to version changes at test time. In LMEB, we treat the API documentation as the corpus and natural language tool-use instructions as queries Q. Note that we use the processed version released by [Shi et al., 2025]. ToolBench [Qin et al., 2024] is large-scale tool-use benchmark constructed from 16,464 real-world RESTful APIs collected from RapidAPI Hub. It provides automatically generated instructions covering both single-tool and multi-tool scenarios, along with annotated solution paths. In LMEB, we treat the API documentation as the corpus and tool-use instructions as queries Q, evaluating procedural retrieval in both single-tool and multi-tool scenarios. Note that we use the processed version released by [Shi et al., 2025]. ReMe [Cao et al., 2025] extracts fine-grained procedural experiences through multi-faceted distillation, supports context-adaptive retrieval via scenario-aware indexing, and maintains compact memory pool through utility-based refinement. Beyond static storage, ReMe highlights retrieval as core mechanism for adapting past solution trajectories to new tasks. In LMEB, we use the ready-to-use memories released for BFCL [Patil et al., 2025] and AppWorld [Trivedi et al., 2024], treating the distilled experiences as the corpus and task and generalized queries as queries Q. Proced_mem_bench [Kohar and Krishnan, 2025] is benchmark that explicitly isolates procedural memory retrieval from task execution, evaluating whether agents can retrieve functionally equivalent procedures under novel object instantiations. Built on ALFWorld [Shridhar et al., 2021], it provides dual corpora of expert and LLM-generated trajectories, along with systematically stratified queries. In LMEB, we treat the trajectory corpus as and procedural task queries as queries Q, where we consider trajectories with relevance score of 7.0 or higher as relevant memories. MemGovern [Wang et al., 2026] introduces procedural memory framework that transforms largescale, unstructured GitHub issue data into agent-usable experiential memory. Through experience governance, it converts historical bug-fixing trajectories into structured experience cards and employs an agentic search strategy for logic-driven retrieval of relevant past solutions. In LMEB, we use the governed experience cards as the corpus and bug-fixing task descriptions as queries Q. DeepPlanning [Zhang et al., 2026] is long-horizon planning benchmark that emphasizes procedural reasoning under local and global constraints. It includes multi-day travel and multi-product shopping tasks, requiring models to retrieve, integrate, and execute action sequences that satisfy time, budget, and resource constraints. For LMEB, we focus on the shopping subset, treating shopping task instructions as queries and the corresponding items as the corpus C.

sections.title: B Tasks
sections.content: In this section, we provide detailed overview of the tasks associated with each dataset under four memory types. As illustrated in Table 10, Table 11, Table 12, and Table 13, each task is characterized by its Task Type, the example abilities assessed. The total number of tasks (#Tasks) for dataset is generally determined by multiplying the number of subsets (#Subsets) by the number of task types, unless otherwise specified. Here, Task Type refers to the specific category of task, reflecting the type of retrieval the model is expected to perform. Subset represents partition of dataset, typically organized by topic, scenario, or source, allowing multiple tasks to be instantiated under different contexts while maintaining consistent evaluation criteria

sections.title: C Instructions
sections.content: To ensure embeddings follow instructions during downstream tasks, we prepend the specific task instructions to the queries. The instructed query is formulated as follows: qinst = Instruct: {task instruction}n Query: q. (1) 20 where denotes the original query; qinst is the instructed query. Task instructions for different datasets as well as task types are summarized in Table 14 and Table 15.

sections.content: The authors of 2 datasets in the LMEB benchmark (REALTALK and TMD) do not specify the dataset license in the paper or repository. We summarize the licenses for the remaining datasets below. EPBench, LongMemEval, MemBench, MLDR, MemGovern: Provided under the MIT License. SciFact: Provided under the CC BY-NC 2.0 license. PeerQA: Provided under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license. NovelQA, ESG-Reports: Data collection archives are under Copyright. SciFact All hematopoietic stem cells segregate their chromosomes randomly. <Title>Datasets ::: AntiScam Dataset</Title> <Text> To enrich available noncollaborative task datasets, we created corpus of human-human anti-scam dialogs in order to learn human elicitation strategies. We chose popular Amazon customer service scam scenario to collect dialogs between users and attackers who aim to collect users information. We posted role-playing task on the Amazon Mechanical Turk platform and collected typing conversation dataset named AntiScam. We collected 220 human-human dialogs. The average conversation length is 12.45 turns and the average utterance length is 11.13 words.
